cafes.name: Onibus Coffee
cafes.vibe: Minimalist timber interior, jazz at just the right volume. The kind of quiet that doesn't feel empty.
cafes.story: I found this place by getting completely lost in Nakameguro at 7am. The barista didn't speak English. I didn't speak Japanese. We communicated entirely through nods and the coffee said everything else. I sat there for two hours and didn't open my phone once.

cafes.name: Café de Flore
cafes.vibe: Every table has a story. Sartre sat here. Hemingway probably argued here. You can feel the weight of it.
cafes.story: Tourist trap? Maybe. But when you're there at 8am before the tour groups arrive, with a crème and a croissant and the whole boulevard to yourself, you understand exactly why this place has survived a hundred years. Some places earn their reputation.

cafes.name: Proud Mary
cafes.vibe: Bright, loud, full of people who take their coffee more seriously than most people take anything.
cafes.story: Melbourne taught me that coffee is a culture, not a beverage. This place had a waitlist at 9am on a Tuesday. I waited 20 minutes and every second was worth it. The flat white was the best I've ever had, and I've had a lot of flat whites.

cafes.name: The Barn
cafes.vibe: Industrial and precise. Like drinking coffee inside a philosophy lecture. In the best way.
cafes.story: Berlin runs on coffee and existential dread and I mean that as a compliment. The Barn is where the serious drinkers go. No oat milk here. No flavour syrups. Just coffee done with the kind of rigour that Germans apply to everything. I respected it.

cafes.name: Blue Tokai — Lado Sarai
cafes.vibe: Home base. Where I come back to. The smell alone is enough.
cafes.story: When I'm back in India and need to think, I come here. Something about the familiarity of it — the same stools, the same roast, the same barista who still doesn't remember my name but always gets my order right. Home doesn't have to be a place. Sometimes it's a cup.

cafes.name: Caffe Sant'Eustachio
cafes.ordered: Gran caffè, with sugar (they put it in, no choice)
cafes.vibe: Standing room only. Tiny cups. Maximum opinion about everything.
cafes.story: They add sugar to the espresso before they give it to you and if you ask them not to, they'll argue with you about it. They're right. I was wrong. It's the best coffee in Rome, possibly Italy, possibly the world, and I say that as someone who doesn't take sugar. Some things are better when you're not in control.
